When negotiating price, going first is usually not the best idea. Anchoring emotions is great for bending their reality, but if you offer a price that is well below what they had in mind, do you end up getting the short end of the stick? You only name price if they are begging you for it. The other advantage of letting the other side name price first is it creates an actual commitment that a deal could be made. It's easier to walk away if no price is named. Researchers have discovered individuals tend to make adjustments from their first point of reference in a psychological quirk known as the anchor and adjustment effect. Though when you let your counterpart anchor you first, you have to make sure you're mentally prepared for the proverbial punch in the nose. Otherwise, when your counterpart hits you with an extreme anchor that you consider outlandish, they will effectively bend your reality, so when you hear their next slightly less absurd offer, it will seem reasonable. Remember, high anchors make deals go away that should have otherwise been made. The Black Swan Group believes this is one of the greatest invisible killers of opportunity.
